coredata.dc:title: Three birds, one stone: Disinfecting and turning waste medical masks into valuable carbon dots for sodium hydrosulfite and Fe3+ detection enabled by a simple hydrothermal treatment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Sen
Hu, Jingyu
Aryee, Aaron Albert
Sun, Yuanqiang
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Disposable medical masks are widely used to prevent respiratory infections due to their ability to block virus particles from entering the human body. The coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic highlighted the importance of medical masks, leading to their widespread use around the world. However, a large number of disposable medical masks have been discarded, some carrying viruses, which have posed a grave threat to the environment and people's health, as well as wasting resources. In this study, a simple hydrothermal method was used for the disinfection of waste medical masks under high-temperature conditions as well as for their transformation into high-value-added carbon dots (CDs, a new type of carbon nanomaterial) with blue-emissive fluorescence, without high energy consumption or environmental pollution. Moreover, the mask-derived CDs (m-CDs) could not only be used as fluorescent probes for sensing sodium hydrosulfite (Na2S2O4), which is widely used in the food and textile industries but is seriously harmful to human health, but also be used for detecting Fe3+ which is harmful to the environment and human health due to its wide use in industries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: COVID-19
Waste medical masks
Carbon dots
Fe3+
Sodium hydrosulfite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of waste medical mask-derived CDs.
Fluorescence detection of Na2S2O4 and Fe3+.
Application of the prepared m-CDs for detecting Na2S2O4.
Application of the prepared m-CDs for detecting Fe3+.
Detection of Na2S2O4 and Fe3+ using m-CDs in real-world samples.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 219741256), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in the University of Henan Province (No. 22TRTSTHN002), 111 Project (D20003, CXJD2021001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, HRTEM (upper inset) image and size distribution (lower inset) of m-CDs (statistical quantity: 49, scale bars: 200 and 2 nm); (b) FTIR spectra of m-CDs; (c) full-scan XPS spectra of m-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of: (d) C 1 s peak; (e) O 1 s peak; (f) UV–vis absorbance (red curve) and fluorescence excitation (blue curve) and emission (black curve) of 0.5 mg/mL of m-CDs in PBS solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of 0.5 mg/mL m-CDs and their response to 20 mM Na2S2O4 in PBS solution (pH = 7.4). Inset: fluorescence color of the m-CDs solution and the change after adding Na2S2O4; (b) fluorescence emission spectrum of 0.5 mg/mL m-CDs treated with different concentrations of Na2S2O4 (0–20 mM) in PBS solution (pH = 7.4); (c) linear relationship between the change in fluorescence intensity of m-CDs, (F0 − F)/F, and various concentrations of Na2S2O4 (0.1–5 mM); (d) change in fluorescence intensity at 450 nm of m-CDs, F/F0, with time after adding 1 mM of Na2S2O4; (e) fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 of 0.5 mg/mL m-CDs in the presence of various species (from left to right): blank; Cys; GSH; Hcy; Na2SO3; Na2S; Na2S2O3; NaHSO3; NaHS; Na2S2O4 (all species were 20 mM); (f) fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 of 0.5 mg/mL m-CDs solution repetitively treated with 20 mM Na2S2O4 and air bubbling (λex/em = 365 nm/450 nm).

(a) Normalized fluorescence intensity of the m-CDs in the presence of various typical metal ions at concentrations of 5 mM under 365 nm excitation; (b) fluorescence spectra of the m-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions (the insets are the optical photographs of the m-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively); (c) fluorescence spectra of the m-CDs treated with Fe3+ solutions of gradually increasing Fe3+ ion concentrations; (d) linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity change (F0 − F)/F with Fe3+ concentrations in the range 1–300 μM.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of waste medical mask-derived carbon dots (m-CDs) and their application to the detection of Fe3+ and Na2S2O4.
Detection of Na2S2O4 in rock candy samples (n = 4).
Detection of Fe3+ in different water samples (n = 4).

head.ce:title: Three birds, one stone: Disinfecting and turning waste medical masks into valuable carbon dots for sodium hydrosulfite and Fe3+ detection enabled by a simple hydrothermal treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Disposable medical masks are widely used to prevent respiratory infections due to their ability to block virus particles from entering the human body. The coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic highlighted the importance of medical masks, leading to their widespread use around the world. However, a large number of disposable medical masks have been discarded, some carrying viruses, which have posed a grave threat to the environment and people's health, as well as wasting resources. In this study, a simple hydrothermal method was used for the disinfection of waste medical masks under high-temperature conditions as well as for their transformation into high-value-added carbon dots (CDs, a new type of carbon nanomaterial) with blue-emissive fluorescence, without high energy consumption or environmental pollution. Moreover, the mask-derived CDs (m-CDs) could not only be used as fluorescent probes for sensing sodium hydrosulfite (Na2S2O4), which is widely used in the food and textile industries but is seriously harmful to human health, but also be used for detecting Fe3+ which is harmful to the environment and human health due to its wide use in industries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Disposable medical masks (classified under personal protective equipment (PPE)) are commonly used for protecting people from respiratory diseases. The production and subsequent use of disposable medical masks in recent times have seen an astronomical increase owing to the outbreak of the coronavirus disease (COVID-19) pandemic in December 2019, which spread rapidly around the world This can be attributed to its ability to effectively inhibit the spread of SARS-CoV-2, It has been reported that about 129 billion disposable medical masks are used globally every month, with an average of 3 million used every minute [1,2]. As the main production center, the daily production of disposable medical masks in China increased 22.5-fold (i.e. from 20 million to 450 million) in just three months (between February and April 2020) according to the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OCED) [3–5]. While protecting our safety, the increased number of disposable medical masks have also caused serious harm to the environment, marine ecosystems, and a huge range of organisms, including humans [6]. According to Oceans Asia, approximately 52 billion masks were produced worldwide in 2020, of which approximately 1.56 billion ended up in our oceans [7]. Since these masks were made mainly of non-degradable materials such as polypropylene (PP) and polyethylene (PE), the large number of masks entering the ocean caused 4,680–6,240 tons of plastic pollution, which could take up to 450 years to decompose, posing a huge threat to marine ecology [8,9]. Therefore, the safe and efficient disposal of waste medical masks is of great importance for the protection of the environment.

Currently, incineration and landfill are commonly used approaches to handling waste medical masks. These methods are likely to cause secondary damage to the environment due to their non-degradability and harmful air pollution produced during combustion [10]. Apart from these, many more waste medical masks are converted into new materials via mechanical and chemical recycling, yet both methods are complicated to operate and consume a lot of energy [11,12]. In addition, the surfaces of waste medical masks are not easily cleaned due to their strong electrostatic interaction with bacteria and viruses, including SARS-CoV-2. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a new method which offers a simple, inexpensive, environment-friendly disposal and reuse of waste medical masks discarded by the general public and healthcare workers.

In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs, a new type of carbon nanomaterial) have shown significant potential in various fields, such as sensing, detecting, environmental monitoring, biological imaging, nanoscopic imaging and cancer diagnosis, because of their excellent features of simple and low-cost synthetic methods, regulated optical characteristics, low toxicity, and superior photostability [13–19]. Various precursors such as organic molecules and biomass have been used to synthesize fluorescent CDs [20–22]. If waste medical masks, as biomass, can be converted into value-added CDs, not only can the environmental pollution caused by them be solved but also waste utilization can be realized. As a commonly used method for preparing fluorescent CDs, the hydrothermal method is highly preferred due to its easy operation and environmentally benign nature. Moreover, waste medical masks are ascribed to plastics waste in that they are made mainly of non-biomass materials such as PP and PE, etc [23]. Based on these facts, we hypothesized that under conditions of high temperature and pressure during hydrothermal treatment, the PP and PE of wasted medical masks could undergo reactions to form fluorescent CDs [24,25]. Furthermore, the effect of high temperature on the activity of SARS-CoV-2 is significant. It has been demonstrated that SARS-CoV-2 can be completely inactivated within 5 min at 100 °C and after more than 45 min at 56 °C [26–33]. Therefore, the virus present in the waste medical masks could be killed during high-temperature processing, thereby achieving the goal of their disinfection and reuse.

To achieve waste utilization and improve resource use efficiency, we explored the ability of waste medical mask-derived CDs (m-CDs) for the detection of sodium hydrosulfite and Fe3+ (see Scheme 1 ). Subsequently, we found that the m-CDs exhibited excellent performance for detecting Na2S2O4 and Fe3+. Na2S2O4 is widely used as a reductive and bleaching agent in the textile and food industries, but is harmful to human health, causing skin diseases, respiratory diseases, and even stomach cancer [34]. The conventional methods for detecting Na2S2O4, including ion chromatography, pulse polarography, and electrochemical methods, are time-consuming or complex processes [35]. However, m-CDs exhibited a rapid response to Na2S2O4, within 10 s, good selectivity and easy handling. Furthermore, m-CDs could also be used as turn-off fluorescent probes for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+, which causes harm to the environment and human health as a result of its use in industrialization and anthropogenic processes. This study provides a new method for environmentally friendly, low-cost, and large-scale treatment of abundant waste medical masks, achieving simultaneous disinfection and high-value-added conversion of abundant m-CDs via a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Moreover, using m-CDs to detect Na2S2O4 and Fe3+ is also good for protecting the environment and human health.

Waste medical masks were obtained from Henan Xiehe Medical Equipment Products Co. Ltd. FeCl3·6H2O was obtained from Anhui Science Technology Co. Ltd. Na2S2O4 was obtained from Saan Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Inc. (St. Louis, MO, USA). KCl, CaCl2, NaCl, CuSO4, AgNO3, MgSO4·7H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Na2SO3, Na2S, Na2S2O3, NaHSO3, NaHS, and Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (D-PBS) solution was obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. All chemicals were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of waste medical mask-derived CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: Waste medical mask-derived CDs were prepared by placing 1.0 g of waste medical mask pieces and 20 mL of deionized water in poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE)-lined autoclaves. After heating at 200 °C for 10 h, the solution was cooled to room temperature. The resulting sample was filtered (0.22 µm filtration membrane) and purified. The solvent was then removed by freeze-drying for 48 h, and the resulting yellow solid was dissolved in DMSO (100 mg/mL) in order to prepare the stock solution. The latter was stored in a sealed glass weighing bottle at room temperature for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Na2S2O4 and Fe3+.
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Na2S2O4 and Fe3+ with m-CDs was accomplished by recording the fluorescence emission spectra at room temperature. In detail, 10.0 μL of prepared m-CD stock solution (100 mg/mL) was dispersed into 2.0 mL of deionized water to form a standard sample with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. To detect Na2S2O4 accurately, the standard sample was placed in a tailor-made vacuum cuvette, and the system was sealed after nitrogen was introduced. Next, different volumes of fresh Na2S2O4 solution (1 M) were added to the airtight system, and the fluorescence spectra were measured after gentle shaking for 10 s. Then, 40 μL of different 1 M solutions of relevant sulfur-containing compounds (Cys, GSH, Hcy, Na2SO3, Na2S, Na2S2O3, NaHSO3, NaHS) were added to 1.96 mL of the m-CD system in order to analyze the selectivity of the m-CDs to Na2S2O4.

Then, different volumes of Fe3+ (20 mM) were added to the prepared standard and a series of fluorescence emission spectra of 0.5 mg/mL standard samples with different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–400 μM) were measured. To explore the selectivity of m-CDs to Fe3+, 1 μL volumes of a series of 10 mM solutions of metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Zn2+) were added to the standard samples in order to estimate their effect on the fluorescence of the m-CDs. The maximum fluorescence intensities at 450 nm excited by 365 nm of the standard sample with and without Fe3+ or Na2S2O4 were recorded as F and F0, respectively.

The m-CDs were synthesized using waste medical masks as precursors via the hydrothermal method. The morphology of the synthesized m-CDs was analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the particle size of the m-CDs varied from 1 to 6 nm, and the average particle size was 3.7 nm. The upper inset high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals their high degree of crystallinity, with a lattice-spacing of approximately 0.39 nm, which coincides closely with the plane of graphitic carbon. To obtain an insight into the functional group properties of m-CDs, we performed Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and the results are shown in Fig. 1b. The strong and broad absorption band at 3475 cm−1 can be attributed to the OH stretching vibration, and the small peak at 1408 cm−1 is an OH bending vibration. The spike at 1698 cm−1 belongs to CC. The stretching vibration peak of CO appears at 1291 cm−1. The peak at 2975 cm−1 corresponds to the CH stretching vibration peak of m-CDs. Furthermore, the elemental content of m-CDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). According to Fig. 1c, the XPS survey scans reveal two characteristic peaks at 282 and 530 eV, corresponding to C 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. The percentage contents of C and O were calculated to be 72.49% and 27.51%, respectively. According to Fig. 1d, the three characteristic peaks of C 1 s are CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.5 eV) and CO (288.3 eV). The two characteristic peaks of O 1 s are attributed to COC/COH (532.3 eV) and CO (534.2 eV) (Fig. 1e). Fig. 1f shows the UV–visible and fluorescence spectra of m-CDs, in which the weak characteristic peak at 322 nm (red curve) can be attributed to n → π* transitions resulting from the conjugation of CO with CC. The main emission band of m-CDs is at 450 nm (black curve), with the excitation wavelength at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the prepared m-CDs for detecting Na2S2O4.
ce:section.ce:para: After the m-CDs were obtained, we first determined the optical response of m-CDs to Na2S2O4 from their fluorescence spectra. To ensure the accuracy of detecting Na2S2O4, fresh Na2S2O4 solution was added to a tailor-made vacuum cuvette containing m-CDs, and nitrogen was introduced into the reaction system to eliminate interference by oxygen. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence spectra of the m-CDs changed significantly upon addition of Na2S2O4, with the fluorescence color changing from blue to colorless under irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp. The results strongly indicated that m-CDs have a clear response to Na2S2O4. To further investigate the ability of m-CDs to detect Na2S2O4, we added different concentrations of freshly prepared Na2S2O4 solution to 0.5 mg/mL m-CD solution under nitrogen protection to determine the correlation between the fluorescence intensity of the m-CDs and the concentration of Na2S2O4 present. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity of the m-CDs decreased gradually as the concentration of Na2S2O4 increased from 0 to 20 mM (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3c reveals that the relative change in fluorescence intensity of the m-CDs at 450 nm, (F0 − F)/F, exhibited a good linear relationship with Na2S2O4 concentration in the range 0.1–5 mM, with a correlation coefficient squared (R2) of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 19.44 μM according to the general 3σ/k method, where σ was the standard deviation of blank sample signal and k was the slope of the linear fit [36]. Additionally, since the Na2S2O4 solution is easily decomposed, the detection time is very important to the accuracy of detection of Na2S2O4. Therefore, the response time of m-CDs to Na2S2O4 was explored by adding 1 mM Na2S2O4 to 0.5 mg/mL m-CDs solution. Fig. S2 indicates that the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs decreased substantially when Na2S2O4 was added, and then decreased gradually until it became stable as the reaction continued. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs at 450 nm exhibited a sharp decrease within 10 s, a gradual decrease from 10 to 60 s, and remained steady from 60 to 120 s, which indicated a rapid response to Na2S2O4. Since the ability of m-CDs to detect Na2S2O4 was based on a reduction reaction between them, we explored the selectivity of m-CDs to determine whether other reducing agents interfered with the detection of Na2S2O4 by m-CDs. According to Fig. 3e, it can be seen that equal concentrations of GSH, Hcy, Na2SO3, Na2S, and Na2S2O3 had a weak effect on the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 of m-CDs, while Cys, NaHSO3, and NaHS had almost no effect, where F and F0 are fluorescence intensities of m-CDs with and without sulfur-containing reducing agents, respectively at 450 nm. Remarkably, only Na2S2O4 reduced the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs sharply when treated with a range of sulfur-containing reducing agents, indicating that m-CDs are highly selective for Na2S2O4. This would be advantageous for the accurate detection of Na2S2O4 in the presence of other reducing agents. Moreover, when the tailor-made vacuum cuvette was opened and oxygen was re-introduced into the reaction system, the fluorescence intensity was recovered. According to Fig. 3e, the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs at 450 nm could still be restored to approximately 95% after six repetitive treatments of adding 20 mM Na2S2O4 and introducing air into 0.5 mg/mL of m-CDs solution. This demonstrated the excellent ability of m-CDs to be used to detect Na2S2O4 reversibly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the prepared m-CDs for detecting Fe3+.
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescent CDs exhibited an excellent ability to chemically sense metal ions due to their good water solubility, superior photoluminescence, and abundant surface functional groups. Due to the ability of the hydroxyl groups on the surface of m-CDs to chelate Fe3+ ions and trigger fluorescence quenching[37], we explored their ability as turn-off fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+. Thus, we investigated the effect of various metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2
a, the fluorescence intensity changed only slightly in the presence of most metal ions, and only Fe3+ triggered dramatic fluorescence quenching. Hence, we further explored the ability of m-CDs to detect Fe3+. Fig. 2b shows that the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs upon the addition of Fe3+ decreased sharply compared with m-CDs without Fe3+ whereas the insets in the figure are the images of m-CDs without and with Fe3+ under 365 nm UV light, which showed the blue fluorescence of m-CDs quenched upon addition of Fe3+. We then explored the fluorescence quenching of m-CDs by different concentrations of Fe3+. According to Fig. 2c, the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs decreased gradually as the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 0 to 450 μM under 365 nm excitation. Fig. 2d revealed the linear relationship between the concentration of Fe3+ and the change in fluorescence intensity (F0 − F)/F in the range 1–300 μM, with a correlation coefficient squared (R2) of 0.998. The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was calculated as 6 (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 11), and k is the slope of the linear curve). Therefore, the m-CDs could be used as a sensitive turn-off fluorescence probe to detect Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Na2S2O4 and Fe3+ using m-CDs in real-world samples.
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine whether our method is practical, different concentrations of Na2S2O4 were added to rock candy solution, according to the standard addition method and detected using m-CDs. In view of the practical application of m-CDs, the standard curve of m-CDs to detect different concentrations of Na2S2O4 were obtained from rock candy solutions in order to accurately evaluate the feasibility of our detection method in practice (Fig. S3). Based on the standard curve, Na2S2O4 was quantified in rock candy solutions, and the recovery rates were obtained. As listed in Table 1 , the recovery rates of the rock candy solution samples were between 94.30% and 101.42%, with their relative standard deviations ranging from 5.18% to 6.73%. These results definitively confirmed the reliability and feasibility of our method for detecting Na2S2O4 in practice.

Similar investigations to verify the practicability of using m-CDs for detecting Fe3+ in real-world water samples, including tap water and lake water were performed. According to the standard curve of Fe3+ detection in real-world water samples (Figs. S4 and S5), the results listed in Table 2 following standard additions showed the recovery rates of the tap water samples were between 95.23% and 100.87%, with their relative standard deviations ranging from 2.64% to 3.23%. The recovery rates of the lake water samples were between 95.59% and 100.61%, with their relative standard deviations ranging from 0.41% to 1.48%. The above results demonstrate that m-CDs can be used for detecting Fe3+ in real-world samples such as tap and lake water.

In summary, following the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic, a large number of disposable medical masks are routinely wasted and cause great damage to the environment and human health. From the perspective of environmental protection and reuse of waste resources, we propose a simple, low-cost, and environment-friendly way to disinfect and turn waste medical masks into fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for detecting Fe3+ and Na2S2O4. The waste medical mask-derived CDs (m-CDs) exhibited selective detection of Fe3+ and Na2S2O4, with wide linear ranges of 1–300 μM and 1–5 mM, respectively, and limits of detection (LODs) of 0.11 and 19.44 μM, respectively. It was also demonstrated that m-CDs could be a valuable tool for detecting Fe3+ and Na2S2O4 in real-world samples. Moreover, detecting Na2S2O4 using m-CDs has been the first application in the field of CDs for detecting Na2S2O4, which broadens the application of fluorescent CDs. This study offers a promising solution to dealing with the large amount of waste disposable medical masks currently produced, by converting them into high-value fluorescent CDs. This method not only relieve their pressure on the ecological environment but can also monitor harmful concentrations of Fe3+ and Na2S2O4 that can adversely affect human health. The m-CDs have potential applications in detecting Fe3 + and Na2S2O4 in the food industry and environmental protection and are expected to realize rapid detection in combination with test strips or polymer beads.

Sen Li: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jingyu Hu: Methodology, Resources. Aaron Albert Aryee: Validation. Yuanqiang Sun: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Zhaohui Li: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 219741256), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in the University of Henan Province (No. 22TRTSTHN002), 111 Project (D20003, CXJD2021001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122659.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stress-induced morphogenic responses: growing out of trouble?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Potters, T.P. Pasternak, Y. Guisez, K.J. Palme, M.A.K. Jansen, Stress-induced morphogenic responses: growing out of trouble?, Trends Plant Sci. 12 (2007) 98–105. Doi: 10.1016/j.tplants.2007.01.004.

Feigl, Indirect effects of COVID-19 on the environment: How plastic contamination from disposable surgical masks affect early development of plants, J. Hazard. Mater. 436 (2022) 129255. Doi: 10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.129255.

Hsueh, Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) and coronavirus disease-2019 (COVID-19): The epidemic and the challenges, Int. J. Antimicrob. Agents. 55 (2020) 105924. Doi: 10.1016/j.ijantimicag.2020.105924.

Doi: 10.1002/alr.22577.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of Discarded Masks on the Offshore Microorganisms during the COVID-19 Pandemic

Wang, Green emitting N,S-co-doped carbon dots for sensitive fluorometric determination of Fe(III) and Ag(I) ions, and as a solvatochromic probe, Microchim. Acta. 185 (2018) 510. Doi: 10.1007/s00604-018-3045-6.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sen Li is currently a MS candidate in pharmaceutical analytical chemistry under the supervision of Professor Zhaohui Li. His research interests are focused on fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications.
Jingyu Hu is currently performed a doctor research at College of Chemistry Zhengzhou University. Her research interests are focused on fluorescent probes and their biological applications.
Aaron Albert Aryee is currently a post-doctor at College of Chemistry Zhengzhou University. His research interests are focused on polymer and their biological applications.

Yuanqiang Sun is an associate professor in the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of fluorescent probes and their biological applications.
Zhaohui Li is a professor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on nanomaterial preparation and their applications in chemo/biosensing.
